speaker: interviewer
text: Today is March 21, 1996. We're interviewing Demetrius Dvoichenko-Markov. My name is Esther Finder We're in Washington, DC and the language will be English. Today is March 21, 1996. We're interviewing Demetrius Dvoichenko-Markov. My name is Esther Finder. We're in Washington, DC and the language will be English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Esther.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to thank you for participating in the interview with us today. Would you please tell me your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Demetrius, D-E-M-E-T-R-I-U-S, Dvoichenko, D-V-O-I-C-H-E-N-K-O, dash Markov, M-A-R-K-O-V.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's your birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birthday is July 10, 1921.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost 75. In July, I will be 75.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Thessaloniki in Greece in the refugee camp. Thessaloniki is spelled T-H-E-S-A-L-O-N-I-K-I, Greece, G-R-E-E-C-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you grow up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I grew up in the Kingdom of Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a guard officer of the Czar in the Crimean guard regiment in Yalta and Livadiya. And he fought as a student, as a volunteer in the Russian-Japanese war, was taken prisoner by the Japanese. He also fought the Balkan Wars as an advisor, a military advisor, to the Bulgarian army in 1912 through 1913 in the war against Turkey. He fought in World War I, and he fought in the Russian Civil War over the White Guard Army. And he fought also in Spain against the Communist and the monarchist forces of the [INAUDIBLE], which are the traditionalist Carlistas. My mother is from the Don Cossack region. Her grandfather was the Marshal of Nobility of the Don Cossack host. She had an academic career. She taught at the University of Bucharest at the Commercial Academy. And then she came to America and she taught in Vassar College, Columbia University, [? Free ?] [? French ?] University, and University of California in Los Angeles. Both my parents are dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one sister. She died recently in 1994. And she lived in Switzerland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was married to a scholar who was active in Turkey. And he disappeared during World War II, so we don't know what happened to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Childhood I spend, in the first six years, I spent in an area which today is called Moldova, or it is also known as Bessarabia. And later on, I went to elementary school in the [? Proesti ?] city which has oil refineries because my father was working in the oil business. And then I went Bucharest. So I was in a German Lutheran school for 12 years till I got my baccalaureate degree. And after that, I had to make my way to join my mother because the divorce court has awarded me to my mother. So the war started already, and I had quite a difficulty to reach the United States. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was because she insisted that my name should be Demetrius because the patron saint of the city of Thessaloniki is Saint Demetrius. My father didn't like the name. He says it's a Greek name. [LAUGHS] But mother won. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have formal religious training?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did have a sacred studies in the school. And our class was divided. We had a rabbi coming to teach the Jewish kids. We had a Catholic priest teaching the Catholic children. And the majority were Lutherans. They had their own pastor coming. And there were only two of us who belonged to the Eastern Orthodox Church, and we had a Romanian priest come to teach us. So we had religious instruction and sacred studies in the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did all these children with different backgrounds get along?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got along fine. It was a great. In fact, I know that some of my Jewish friends are now in Palestine. They were able to escape from Romania because the Romanian dictator protected the Jews against the Germans. So we-- we got fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any anti-Semitism at all when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as soon as the-- the German kids had to join the Hitler Youth, then you did have, definitely, anti-Semitism. I continued my friendship with my Jewish boys, but the-- I was condemned for that. And I was given silent treatment for two years because I dared to continued to be friends with my Jewish friends that I grew up with. [LAUGHS] So there was, yes. There was definitely anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of things did you do for fun when you were a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: We enjoyed seeing movies. Movies was a big entertainment for us, particularly American movies. That was very popular. And a Sunday treat would be to go to the movies. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were a child, what were your plans for the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: I applied to the University of Leipzig in Germany because most of graduates of my school, they went to study in Germany. But that was interrupted by the war and because I had to join my mother in America. And-- so I-- I had to change my plans. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about America. Tell me about coming over here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was difficult to come, because from the Kingdom of Romania, I had to get a transit visa through Hungary. And I had to get a transit visa through Switzerland, and through Vichy, France. I did obtain the Hungarian visa. I did obtain the Hungarian and even the Swiss visa. But Vichy refused to give me the visa, and I couldn't reach Spain.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to the ambassador-- Spanish ambassador. And I told him, well I can't get to Madrid and continue my trip to America. He says, wait a minute. I'm going to go and see the ambassador of Nazi Germany, but you better keep your story straight. You tell the Germans that you are a Spanish student returning to Spain, because I did have a Spanish passport. And so he went to Baron von Killinger, and he wired to Berlin, and I got a visa through occupied Poland where civilians were not allowed in '41. And it was quite difficult. In order to protect me, the Spanish ambassador gave me a diplomatic package to take to the Spanish embassy in Berlin. So when I got to the train, people were on the roof. I couldn't get even in the third class wagon. I couldn't get in. So the military attache of the Spanish embassy said, well this is a diplomatic courier. And could you fix him with a seat? So they put me in first class, even if I had a ticket for third class. So I traveled quite comfortably all the way to the border of the Kingdom of Romania. And I had to get off the train and cross into occupied Poland. And suddenly, a German appeared with a bayonet and a rifle, and halt. What are you doing here? Civilians are not allowed here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, but I do have a visa. I took my passport, showed him the visa. He says, that's unusual. Open your suitcase. So I opened suitcase. On top, I had the diplomatic package.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he looked at. What is that? I said, well, diplomatic package I'm taking to the Spanish embassy in Berlin. Oh, first class. He put me in first class. And they were the lowest ranking officers there. The Germans Wehrmacht major, and all the way up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And naturally, the Germans right away ask me who I am. And I say, well, I'm a Spanish student returning to Spain. I didn't tell them I'm going to America. And there was one officer there who says, oh, I married a Spanish girl. I was a volunteer in Spain. I just learned Spanish. I knew only a few words. I say, [SPEAKING SPANISH]. He says, but the others don't know Spanish. You speak German so fluently, let's continue in German. So that was a close one. The one thing that I will never forget is, when we stopped at the city of Krakow, the German Red Cross came on board, and they brought ersatz coffee, that was chicory, and a piece of German bread. And everybody in uniform got a cup of this hot chicory coffee and bread. And the Red Cross girl looked at me. I was a kid sitting there. A civilian, naturally. And she didn't know what to do. Well, I kept quiet. And she gave me the hot coffee and the bread. I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. She says, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And I didn't have anything hot for about three weeks, so it was very nice to drink something hot. And when I looked at the station platform, there was a boy that looked like a skeleton. You probably have seen those pictures of World War II. Just skin and bones. And he had some Polish newspapers. And I waved at him and said, please come closer. And he understood the international sign language.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he came closer, and I gave him the bread. He tore into that bread. It seems he was really starving. And then I heard a voice back of me, how dare you to give our German bread to this subhuman. I can arrest you for that. Who are you anyway? I turned around, and it was an SS obersturmbannfuhrer with the Jolly Roger, you know, the regular SS man. And I said, well, I'm a Spanish student returning to Spain. Oh, Spain. Well it's all right. But you better watch. Don't you know anything about the master race and the subhumans? I didn't tell him I was a subhuman, too. But that was-- so it was a close call. I got a good lecture on the master race that wants to rule the whole world. And then I went through-- got a special visa to go through occupied France. And while I was able to cross into Spain, and on the train, I couldn't understand people speaking, because they didn't speak Spanish. And when the conductor came, [SPEAKING SPANISH], I said, oh, you speak Spanish. Well, what are they talking? I can't understand a word. He said, they are Basques. They are talking the Basque language. So that was my contact with the Basques. It was very interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Madrid, I got stuck for a whole year waiting for the visa and for transportation. HIAS did help me to get to the visa and to get transportation. There were only two Spanish boats crossing the Atlantic. It was war in '42. And finally, I got a ticket on one of the boats, [INAUDIBLE], I remember. And when the time came to board the ship, I came with my suitcases to get in. I had the American visa, immigration visa to join my mother. And the captain says, well, you can leave your baggage here, but you have to get the British visa. I say, I'm not going to Great Britain. I'm going to New York, the United States. He says, I can't take you on board without the British permission because Britannia rules the waves. So you have to go to the British consulate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to the British consulate. And British looked at my passport and says, this is not a Spanish name. How come you have a Spanish passport? I say, well, I'm a minor. And according to international law, you have to have the citizenship of your father. And father did obtain Spanish citizenship after the Spanish Civil War. So that's how I became Spanish citizen. Well, but what were you doing in Romania? I had to go through the whole thing. And they says, what did you see in Poland? So they kept me a whole day, from 8 o'clock in the morning all the way to 5 o'clock, the closing time, asking me what did I see in occupied Poland. What did I see in Berlin? What did I see in occupied France?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't even offer me a cup of tea. I was hungry, and a growing boy, naturally. So as soon as I got out, I-- I went to eat. But everything in Spain was rationed at that time. So at least a little thing, I had something to eat. And finally, I was able to get on board. Our ship looked-- was all lighted up. And they had written-- written all over, neutral Spain. And we had submarines coming close to our ship. We were bugged by Allied airplanes. We had typhus epidemics on board because the food was so bad. Five passages died. We got a special permit to land in Trinidad to bury the passengers. In Havana, we were not allowed to go on land because we were quarantined. The same thing happened when we arrived in New Orleans. In New Orleans, we also were under quarantine. And then finally, they-- they let us out, and we were interrogated by military-- American military officials. Again, I was interrogated the whole day. They were asking me what did I see in Nazi occupied territories. And-- but this time, when I finally got out from the interrogation, the Red Cross was waiting and they had coffee. They had donuts. They had some chocolate, which I didn't see for several years. [LAUGHS] So the Red Cross was very nice. They even had a volunteer professor from the University of Louisiana who asked me whether I know how to buy the ticket and how to get on the train. I said no, I don't know New Orleans.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, well, what languages do you speak? So I said, well, eight. Take your pick. So this professor was a professor of French language. So we start talking in French. And because my English was kind of poor-- I had only two years of English in school. And he introduced me to the hot dog. And he helped me to get a ticket, to send a telegram to my mother. And when I got on the train, one railroad car was empty. So I thought, oh gee, I can make myself comfortable here. So the conductor came in. You can't sit here. You have to go to the other car which was crowded. I didn't know why, so as soon as I got to the other car, I asked the passenger, why did he chase me out of there? He says, well, this is for the blacks. You are not allowed to be in that particular-- particular car. You have to go into this car. OK. So we arrived in Washington, DC. And we had to change trains. A very pretty Red Cross girl was waiting for us. And she took us to the other train, make sure that we got-- and our group that was going to New York, we arrived. And my mother finally did meet me. I remember that I didn't have milk for several years, and my mother had a carton of milk in a Frigidaire. I polished the whole thing. And I say, oh my goodness, is the milk rationed? She says, no, here in the United States it's not rationed. Oh, how wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then she says, well, I will show you New York. Let's go. I say, oh, I have to take my passport because they always stop and check. She says no, not here in New York. Nobody checks. You don't need your passport. So it was a very great impression of America, you know, the free country. And I-- I got even the greetings from my neighbor. I arrived in October, and in December, I got greetings from the neighbors. I said, gee, what a wonderful country. I just arrived, and they send me greetings.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then there was a small print report to the US Army in December, and so I was drafted. And I had a hard time to understand the English language, because what I studied, I studied Shakespeare, Thackeray, and so on-- the English literature. And here, the sergeant said, right face. So I turn my face right. So the sergeant got awfully mad. Soldier, are you trying to sabotage the US Army? One week, KP. Well, I learned the hard way. Then they saw that I'm kind of funny. They send me to a battalion of an alphabets, you know. They gave me a written exam. I passed it with flying colors. They say, what are you doing here? I say, well, I don't understand the slang. You know, I don't understand what right face is, left face is. So what. So you are a problem. I was in Camp Lee, Virginia where they had that Southern accent. But I didn't want to get a Southern accent. I went to the movies every evening to learn the King's English. And so they say, well, you still have to take your basic training. So what we're going to do in the evening, when you return from your training, you contact the sergeant and give him a list of words that you don't understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had always a list of words, and he did explain the meaning of those words. So finally, I-- I did assimilate. And then they sent me to a school, to Camp Ritchie, a military intelligence school. And we had a sergeant there from-- a Georgia cracker who took 30 years to become a sergeant. And he hated college boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he would tell us, you see that rock? Roll it over there, then roll it back. Pick up those cigarette butts. KP, latrine duty, Oh, he really had fun. We had to wait to go to school. And there was a course of two years, a course which was concentrated in two months. And I passed it with about 85 grade, because I still had trouble to understand some technical word. And I was promoted right away to sergeant. Yes. Give me a time frame. When is this happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is happening in 1942. In 1942. In 1943, I graduated. And they took our group and said, you have to be interpreters because we have Soviet Air Force in Alaska, and we need interpreters. And so you're going to be an interpreter. But you never reveal that you are part of the American military intelligence. You-- we give you a uniform of quartermaster. And another fellow in my office had the uniform of infantry. And when we got to Seattle to go on a troop ship, a young officer was reading the roaster. When he came to our group, he yelled, in a loud voice, military intelligence.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got on board, the American soldiers didn't understand what military intelligence is, you know. And nobody would talk to us. So we were really isolated. I had the most miserable trip on a ship to Alaska because nobody would talk to me because they were afraid. They didn't know exactly that we are there only to collect information, but nothing else. So that was a miserable trip to Alaska. Alaska was very interesting because when I met, for the first time, Soviet pilots. They say, oh, you speak Russian so fluently. Where did you learn? Were you born in the Soviet Union? I say, no, god forbid, no. I wasn't born there. I was born in Greece. But how did you learn? So I told them who my father is. And my family background. They say, oh, you're a white bandit. So first time in my life I was called a white bandit. So I said, pardon me, we are white guards. You are the red bandits who took over. So there was a proper introduction. And we did work all right for two years.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing is that they were awfully suspicious of me. They invited to a party to celebrate the Battle of Stalingrad. And they invited all American interpreters. And they had a whole barrel of black caviar. Wow. And then they had vodka, naturally. And they had a big glass that we used for cocktails. And they poured the vodka all the way to the top. And they took a wooden spoon and put it in my plate, a whole pile of black caviar. I looked at that. Oh, gee, that's delicious. They say, no, first you have to drink. Bottoms up. I say, wait a minute. I was the youngest in the outfit. They wouldn't even serve me beer, you know, when I went. So I had to grow a mustache to-- to get beer. And here, they wanted me to drink a whole big-- I said-- I said, no I can't do that. Ooh, we have to take a toast. I say, well, I just take a little bit to taste it. And I start coughing, and sneezing, and so on. I said, I can't do it. So they got mad at me. They say, aha. You want us to get drunk so you can spy on us. My commanding officer got worried. International incident, here. They-- they-- they called me a spy. So he said, Sergeant, come over here. I have a message for headquarters. And he gave me a white piece of paper and you better-- So I looked at that caviar and never tasted it. So I had to go, get out. So-- so that was one painful incident, but otherwise, it was all right. It was interesting to-- to work with the Soviets. To see the other world. [LAUGHS] It was very interesting. We know that one incident that I-- I really enjoyed was when Stalin gave the order to have the shoulder-- what do you call it, boards, or something. You know, they have those. And that was a symbol for imperial Russia. My father did wear that when he was fighting against the Communists. And my uncle was tortured to death by the Communists by taking big nails and nailing this into-- that's how the Communists hated this symbol. And Stalin reintroduced this symbol. And all the pilots did obey the order, but there was one major who refused to wear them. So he held out for a whole month. We knew he was a political commissar. [LAUGHS] But later on, sheepishly, he put them on. [LAUGHS] That was also another incident in Alaska we had. It was very interesting.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then, after I got out of Alaska, my mother had an operation, and I had to get a job. So I went to Washington, DC, shopping around and contacting my friends. And they say, look, they need linguists at the Nuremberg war crime trials. Why don't you apply for that? Again, I'm going to ask you, can put this in time perspective for me. How long were you in Alaska?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until '45, when the war was over. Yeah. When-- after victory in Europe, and after victory in Japan. Because when victory in Europe happened, they mobilized us and they told us that we are going to go to Siberia, to the city of [INAUDIBLE] to be interpreters there, because Japan had to be defeated. And the Soviet Union did declare war on Japan. And I didn't look forward to go to Siberia. When I hear the words of Siberia, its kind of-- [LAUGHS] but fortunately, right away, VJ Day happened. And then we were able to be discharged [LAUGHS] in '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came to Washington, and-- [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Applied for a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's pause here and change the tape.
